# UC-22.2.54 · NIS2 Art.31-35 supervision, enforcement, fine exposure and GDPR coordination evidence

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Security &middot; **Type:** Compliance, Security &middot; **Wave:** Run &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We keep watch on official requests from regulators and the paperwork that answers them, so nothing important crosses its deadline quietly. We also help spot when a personal-data incident might touch the same storyline, so lawyers see both sides early.*

---

## Description

Surfaces overdue supervisory audit findings and non-closed ServiceNow GRC policy tasks whose descriptions reference inspections or articles so legal can see evidence request backlogs before penalties or GDPR overlaps escalate.

## Value

Executives maintain an operational cockpit for authority interactions—response SLAs, open remediation, and linked privacy incidents—without claiming that any Splunk panel replaces counsel’s interpretation of fines or jurisdiction.

## Implementation

(1) Land `grc:audit_finding` via your GRC tool’s connector. (2) Map ServiceNow policy table through Splunkbase 1928. (3) Optional: ingest DPO breach tickets to `index=privacy`. (4) Daily alert; route to GC office. (5) Quarterly export to `index=audit_evidence`.

## Evidence

Scheduled `UC-22.2.54` saved search output, underlying `grc:audit_finding` and `snow:sn_grc_policy` excerpts, optional `index=privacy` references, and immutable copy in `index=audit_evidence`.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

Insert a synthetic `grc:audit_finding` event with `remediation_status=open` and `due_date` seven days in the past plus a matching `competent_authority` code, then confirm the search flags `supervision_gap=1` for that `finding_id` within the hourly run.

### Negative scenario

When findings are `closed` before the due date and there are no open `snow:sn_grc_policy` tasks matching the supervisory keyword filter, the production alert remains silent for an entire seven-day backtest window aside from known test fixtures kept in a dev index.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- This UC covers NIS2 Art.31-35 — the supervision and enforcement chapter. These articles define how competent authorities supervise essential and important entities: Art.31 covers general supervisory powers (on-site inspections, audits, document requests), Art.32 covers specific supervisory measures for essential entities (binding instructions, compliance orders), Art.33 covers supervisory measures for important entities (ex post supervision), Art.34 covers administrative fines (up to EUR 10 million or 2% of global annual turnover for essential entities, EUR 7 million or 1.4% for important entities), and Art.35 covers coordination between NIS2 enforcement and GDPR enforcement when an incident involves personal data.
- **What Splunk can and cannot do here**: Splunk cannot compute fines, predict enforcement outcomes, or file formal responses to supervisory authorities. What Splunk CAN do is: (1) track overdue supervisory audit findings so the organisation responds within deadlines, (2) surface open GRC policy tasks related to supervisory activities so nothing falls through the cracks, (3) correlate NIS2 incidents with GDPR breach notifications to ensure Art.35 coordination between the CISO and DPO, and (4) provide an immutable audit trail proving that all supervisory interactions were handled within SLA. This UC is primarily a governance evidence tool, not a technical detection — its audience is the General Counsel and CISO, not the SOC.
- **Data architecture**: the SPL processes two sourcetypes from two indexes:
  - `index=grc sourcetype=grc:audit_finding` — emitted by the GRC platform (ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, MetricStream, OneTrust) after a supervisory audit finding is recorded. Key fields: `finding_id` (unique identifier, e.g., "FIND-2026-NIS2-001"), `due_date` (ISO 8601 — the remediation deadline set by the competent authority or internal audit), `competent_authority` (the supervisory body, e.g., "NSM" for Norway, "BSI" for Germany, "ANSSI" for France), `remediation_status` (open, in_progress, closed, accepted_risk), `severity` (critical, high, medium, low), `finding_description` (text of the finding).
  - `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy` — ServiceNow GRC policy table records. These represent policy attestation tasks, supervisory response tasks, and compliance remediation items. Key fields: `number` (ServiceNow record number, e.g., "SNGRC0001234"), `short_description` (task description — the SPL searches for keywords like "supervisory", "inspect", "nis2"), `state` (ServiceNow state code: 1=New, 2=In Progress, 3=On Hold, 5=Resolved, 6=Closed, 7=Cancelled — only state 6 indicates complete closure), `due_date`, `assigned_to`, `assignment_group`.
- **Optional GDPR coordination data**: `index=privacy sourcetype=gdpr:breach_notification` with fields: `breach_id` (unique DPA reference), `dpo_signoff` (yes/no), `notification_date`, `breach_category` (confidentiality, integrity, availability), `nis2_linked_incident` (the NIS2 incident ID if the breach also triggers Art.23 notification). Art.35 requires coordination between NIS2 authorities and GDPR supervisory authorities when a security incident also constitutes a personal data breach. By linking NIS2 incident IDs with GDPR breach IDs, this UC proves that the CISO and DPO coordinated their responses.
- **Lookups**:
  - `nis2_supervision_kfp_exceptions.csv` with columns: `row_key` (finding_id or ServiceNow number), `exception_until` (ISO 8601 date), `reason` (e.g., "negotiated extension with BSI until 2026-06-30", "counsel review pending"). This handles findings where the competent authority has granted a formal extension, or where counsel has placed a legal hold.
  - `nis2_competent_authorities.csv` with columns: `authority_code` (e.g., "NSM"), `authority_name` ("Nasjonal sikkerhetsmyndighet"), `jurisdiction` ("Norway"), `contact_email`, `escalation_sla_days` (the number of days after which an overdue finding must be escalated to the board). Optional enrichment lookup.
- **Index retention**: `index=grc` ≥ 7 years (supervisory findings may be referenced in enforcement proceedings years later). `index=itsm` ≥ 2 years. `index=privacy` ≥ 7 years (GDPR breach records). `index=audit_evidence` ≥ 7 years.
- **RBAC**: role `nis2_legal_ops` with `srchIndexesAllowed = grc, itsm, privacy, audit_evidence`. This is highly sensitive data — supervisory findings, enforcement correspondence, and GDPR breach records are legally privileged in many jurisdictions. Access must be restricted to the General Counsel office, CISO, and DPO. Field-level masking should be applied to `finding_description` for non-legal roles to prevent accidental exposure of supervisory correspondence.
- **GDPR considerations**: the `index=privacy` data contains personal data breach details. Under GDPR Art.33(5), breach documentation must be maintained but access-controlled. Ensure that Splunk RBAC prevents SOC analysts from accessing `index=privacy` — only the DPO and legal team should have access. Consider field-level encryption for `data_subjects_affected` and `breach_description` fields.

### Step 1 — Configure data collection
**GRC audit finding data:**
If using ServiceNow GRC, the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928) can pull audit findings from the `sn_audit_finding` or `sn_grc_item` table. Configure a modular input:
- Table: `sn_audit_finding` (or your GRC module's finding table)
- Filter: `active=true` (only active findings)
- Poll interval: 300 seconds
- Index: `grc`
- Sourcetype: `grc:audit_finding`

For other GRC platforms (Archer, OneTrust, MetricStream), configure a REST API or file-based export to HEC:
```json
{
  "event": {
    "finding_id": "FIND-2026-NIS2-001",
    "due_date": "2026-06-30",
    "competent_authority": "NSM",
    "remediation_status": "open",
    "severity": "high",
    "finding_description": "Inadequate network segmentation between OT and IT environments"
  },
  "sourcetype": "grc:audit_finding",
  "index": "grc"
}
```

Verify:
```
index=grc sourcetype=grc:audit_finding earliest=-60d
| stats count dc(finding_id) as findings dc(competent_authority) as authorities values(remediation_status) as statuses
```
Expected: `findings` matches the number of active supervisory findings in your GRC platform. `authorities` shows the supervisory bodies you interact with.

**ServiceNow GRC policy tasks:**
Verify the `sn_grc_policy` table input is active in the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow:
```
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy (short_description="*supervisory*" OR short_description="*inspect*" OR short_description="*nis2*") earliest=-60d
| stats count dc(number) as tasks values(state) as states
```
Expected: `tasks` matches the number of open supervisory-related GRC policy items in ServiceNow. `states` should include values like 1 (New), 2 (In Progress), and ideally 6 (Closed) for completed items.

**GDPR breach correlation (optional but recommended for Art.35):**
If your DPO maintains breach records in a separate system, ingest them to `index=privacy sourcetype=gdpr:breach_notification`. The critical linkage field is `nis2_linked_incident` — when a security incident triggers both NIS2 Art.23 notification and GDPR Art.33 notification, this field links them. This allows the UC to prove that both notification streams were handled in coordination.

Verify:
```
index=privacy sourcetype=gdpr:breach_notification earliest=-365d
| stats count dc(breach_id) as breaches dc(nis2_linked_incident) as nis2_linked
```

**Lookup configuration:**
Place `nis2_supervision_kfp_exceptions.csv` and `nis2_competent_authorities.csv` in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA-nis2-legal/lookups/`. Define in `transforms.conf`:
```ini
[nis2_supervision_kfp_exceptions]
filename = nis2_supervision_kfp_exceptions.csv
case_sensitive_match = false

[nis2_competent_authorities]
filename = nis2_competent_authorities.csv
case_sensitive_match = false
```

### Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.54 NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence`.

#### Understanding this SPL — design decisions:

- **Dual-sourcetype union**: the search combines GRC audit findings and ServiceNow GRC policy tasks in a single result set. These represent two different aspects of supervisory compliance: (a) findings from audits (what the authority found wrong), and (b) policy tasks (the organisation's response and remediation actions). Together, they provide a complete picture of the supervisory interaction lifecycle.

- **`supervision_gap` logic**: `remediation_status IN ("open","in_progress") AND now()>due_epoch` flags findings that are overdue. The finding is not closed, and the due date has passed. This is the most critical condition — an overdue supervisory finding can trigger enforcement action under Art.32 (binding instructions) or Art.34 (administrative fines). The severity of the finding determines the urgency: a critical/high finding overdue by 30+ days is an escalation-level event.

- **`policy_gap` logic**: `state!="6" AND match(short_description,"(?i)supervisory|inspect|nis2")` identifies ServiceNow GRC tasks that are related to supervisory activities but have not been closed. State 6 is "Closed" in ServiceNow — other states (New, In Progress, On Hold, Resolved) indicate the task is not fully complete. The keyword match on `short_description` filters to only supervisory-relevant tasks. Tune the keywords based on your organisation's ServiceNow naming conventions.

- **`row_key=coalesce(finding_id,number)`**: normalises the unique identifier across both sourcetypes. GRC findings use `finding_id`, ServiceNow tasks use `number`. The `coalesce` creates a single key for the exception register lookup.

- **Exception register with legal holds**: the exception lookup handles a critical business requirement — some findings have negotiated extensions with the competent authority (e.g., "BSI granted 90-day extension until 2026-09-30"), and others are under legal review (counsel has placed a hold on remediation pending interpretation of the finding). These should not trigger alerts until the extension expires or the hold is lifted.

Schedule: cron `0 7 * * 1-5` (weekdays at 07:00). Time range: embedded `earliest=-60d` in the SPL. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`.

Alert actions: (1) For findings with `severity=critical` and `supervision_gap=1`: email to the General Counsel with finding details and days overdue. (2) For findings overdue by more than 30 days: escalate to the CISO and board secretary. (3) Write all results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=nis2:supervision:evidence`.

Extended search — GDPR coordination check (Art.35):
```spl
index=privacy sourcetype=gdpr:breach_notification earliest=-90d
| where isnotnull(nis2_linked_incident)
| join type=left nis2_linked_incident
    [search index=notable sourcetype=stash earliest=-90d
     | rename search_name as nis2_uc, rule_name as incident_type
     | table nis2_linked_incident nis2_uc incident_type]
| eval coordination_gap=if(dpo_signoff!="yes", 1, 0)
| where coordination_gap=1
| table breach_id nis2_linked_incident breach_category notification_date dpo_signoff
```
This surfaces GDPR breaches linked to NIS2 incidents where the DPO has not signed off on coordination. Art.35 requires that when a competent authority discovers a personal data breach during NIS2 supervision, it must inform the GDPR supervisory authority. The DPO sign-off field proves this coordination happened.

### Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Overdue finding detection**: create a synthetic `grc:audit_finding` event with `remediation_status=open` and `due_date` set to 7 days ago. POST to HEC. Run the search. Confirm `supervision_gap=1` for that `finding_id`. Change `remediation_status=closed`. POST again (newer timestamp). Run the search. Confirm the finding no longer appears.

(b) **Policy task detection**: in ServiceNow, create a test GRC policy task with `short_description="NIS2 supervisory response test"` and `state=2` (In Progress). Wait for the Splunk Add-on poll. Run the search. Confirm `policy_gap=1` for that task. Close the task (state=6). Wait for the next poll. Run again. Confirm no appearance.

(c) **Exception register test**: add the test finding's `finding_id` to `nis2_supervision_kfp_exceptions.csv` with `exception_until=<tomorrow>`. Run the search. Confirm the finding does not appear. Set `exception_until=<yesterday>`. Run again. Confirm it reappears.

(d) **State code verification**: verify that ServiceNow state codes match the SPL filter. Run: `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy | stats count by state`. Map each numeric state to its label in ServiceNow (Administration > System Properties > Dictionary > task > state). Confirm that state 6 is indeed "Closed" in your instance (some organisations customise state values).

(e) **GDPR coordination test**: if using the extended search, create a test `gdpr:breach_notification` event with `nis2_linked_incident="INC0012345"` and `dpo_signoff=no`. Run the extended search. Confirm `coordination_gap=1`. Change `dpo_signoff=yes`. Run again. Confirm no appearance.

(f) **Competent authority enrichment**: if using the `nis2_competent_authorities.csv` lookup, verify enrichment: `index=grc sourcetype=grc:audit_finding | lookup nis2_competent_authorities.csv authority_code AS competent_authority OUTPUT authority_name jurisdiction`. Confirm `authority_name` and `jurisdiction` are populated for all findings.

### Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 Supervisory Compliance`):
- **Row 1 — Risk exposure**: Single-value `Overdue findings` (red ≥ 1). Single-value `Open policy tasks` (amber ≥ 5). Single-value `GDPR-linked incidents without DPO signoff` (red ≥ 1 — from extended search). Single-value `Days until next SLA deadline` (from the earliest `due_date` among open findings).
- **Row 2 — Finding SLA table**: sortable table of all findings with `finding_id`, `competent_authority`, `due_date`, `days_overdue` (calculated), `remediation_status`, `severity`, `assigned_to`. Colour-code: red for overdue + critical/high, amber for overdue + medium/low, green for within SLA. This is the primary artifact for board reporting.
- **Row 3 — Timeline**: `index=grc sourcetype=grc:audit_finding | timechart span=1mon dc(finding_id) as new_findings dc(eval(if(remediation_status="closed",finding_id,null()))) as closed_findings`. Line chart showing the inflow and outflow of supervisory findings over 12 months. The gap between the two lines is the backlog.
- **Row 4 — Authority breakdown**: pie chart of open findings by `competent_authority`. Shows which supervisory bodies the organisation has the most outstanding interactions with. Useful for the General Counsel to prioritise responses.
- **Row 5 — GDPR coordination matrix** (if Art.35 data is available): table of all GDPR breaches linked to NIS2 incidents with `breach_id`, `nis2_linked_incident`, `dpo_signoff`, `notification_date`. Shows the DPO whether coordination is complete for each dual-triggered incident.

Runbook (owner: General Counsel / CISO):
1. Daily (weekdays): review the alert. For each overdue finding:
   - Check the GRC platform for current remediation status. If progress has been made but the status hasn't been updated in the GRC system, update it.
   - For findings overdue by 1-14 days: the remediation owner should provide a revised timeline. Document the reason for the delay.
   - For findings overdue by 15-30 days: escalate to the CISO. The competent authority may follow up — prepare a status update.
   - For findings overdue by 30+ days: escalate to the board. Art.34 fines can be imposed for non-compliance with supervisory measures under Art.32. The board must be aware of the financial exposure.
2. Monthly: present the finding SLA table and timeline to the CISO and General Counsel. Include: new findings received, findings closed, average remediation time, and backlog trend.
3. After any NIS2 incident notification (Art.23): check the GDPR coordination extended search. If the incident involved personal data, verify that the DPO was notified and has signed off on the GDPR Art.33 notification. Document the coordination in both the NIS2 incident record and the GDPR breach record.
4. Quarterly: archive the dashboard as PDF, the finding SLA table as CSV, and the GDPR coordination matrix as CSV. Sign with RFC3161. Store in the compliance vault alongside the formal supervisory correspondence.

### Step 5 — Troubleshooting

- **Null `due_date` in GRC findings.** Some GRC platforms don't populate `due_date` for all findings (e.g., informational findings without remediation deadlines). The `strptime` returns null, and `now()>due_epoch` evaluates to false, so these findings are correctly excluded from the `supervision_gap` condition. However, findings that SHOULD have a due date but don't are silently ignored. Fix: add a complementary check: `| eval missing_due=if(remediation_status IN ("open","in_progress") AND isnull(due_date), 1, 0)` and include `missing_due=1` as an additional alert condition.

- **ServiceNow `state` codes differ from default.** ServiceNow state fields can be customised. If your instance uses different state values (e.g., state 8 for "Closed" instead of 6), the SPL's `state!="6"` filter will produce false positives (treating closed tasks as open). Fix: verify your instance's state mapping: `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy | stats count by state | sort state`. Map the numeric values to labels and update the SPL accordingly.

- **Keyword match too broad — false positive GRC tasks.** The regex `match(short_description,"(?i)supervisory|inspect|nis2")` catches tasks with "inspect" in any context (e.g., "Inspect fire extinguisher" or "Quality inspection procedure"). Fix: narrow the regex: `match(short_description,"(?i)(supervisory audit|regulatory inspect|nis2 compliance|competent authority)")`. Or maintain a lookup of relevant ServiceNow task numbers and filter against that instead of keyword matching.

- **GRC platform sends duplicate findings.** Some GRC tools create a new record each time a finding is updated, rather than updating the existing record. This produces multiple `finding_id` entries with different `remediation_status` values. The SPL uses `where` (not `stats`), so all versions appear. Fix: add `| dedup finding_id sortby -_time` after the union to keep only the latest version of each finding.

- **GDPR breach records not linked to NIS2 incidents.** The `nis2_linked_incident` field is populated manually by the DPO or incident manager. If they forget to link the records, the Art.35 coordination check misses the connection. Fix: create a supplementary check that looks for temporal and IP-based correlation: `index=notable earliest=-7d | join type=left src [search index=privacy sourcetype=gdpr:breach_notification | rename affected_system as src]`. This heuristic correlation surfaces potential Art.35 overlaps that haven't been formally linked.

- **Fine exposure calculation request.** Executives may ask "what is our current fine exposure?" NIS2 Art.34 fines are calculated as percentages of global annual turnover, which is outside Splunk's scope. However, you can provide a proxy: count of overdue critical/high findings multiplied by a risk-weighted score. This is NOT a fine calculation — clearly label it as "risk exposure index" and include a disclaimer that actual fine calculations require legal analysis.

- **Legal privilege concerns.** Supervisory correspondence and findings may be legally privileged. Splunk data can be subpoenaed or requested during litigation. Ensure: (a) `index=grc` and `index=privacy` have strict RBAC, (b) the `finding_description` field is not included in full-text searches that non-legal users can run, (c) the dashboard does not display the full text of findings — only `finding_id`, `due_date`, and `remediation_status`. Consult with the General Counsel on data retention and privilege implications before deploying this UC.


## SPL

```spl
(index=grc sourcetype=grc:audit_finding earliest=-60d)
       OR (index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy earliest=-60d)
| eval due_epoch=strptime(due_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval supervision_gap=if(sourcetype="grc:audit_finding" AND remediation_status IN ("open","in_progress") AND isnotnull(due_epoch) AND now()>due_epoch,1,0)
| eval policy_gap=if(sourcetype="snow:sn_grc_policy" AND state!="6" AND match(short_description,"(?i)supervisory|inspect|nis2"),1,0)
| where supervision_gap=1 OR policy_gap=1
| eval row_key=coalesce(finding_id,number)
| lookup nis2_supervision_kfp_exceptions row_key OUTPUT exception_until
| where isnull(exception_until) OR now()>strptime(exception_until,"%Y-%m-%d")
| table _time finding_id competent_authority due_date remediation_status number short_description supervision_gap policy_gap
```

## Visualization

SLA breach table by `competent_authority`, timeline of tasks, heatmap of GDPR-tagged linked records.

## Known False Positives

• **Negotiated extension with regulator** — Distinguish: `finding_id` tagged `extension_granted` in GRC. Suppress: exception register with PDF hash reference.
• **Duplicate ServiceNow tasks after clone** — Distinguish: same `finding_id` twice. Suppress: `| dedup finding_id`.
• **Privacy breach record unrelated supervisory topic** — Distinguish: `breach_category` not crossing cyber incident. Suppress: filter `where breach_linkage="nis2_related"`.
• **Year-end closure codified offline** — Distinguish: counsel notes mailed; Splunk stale. Suppress: time-bound `lookup` until systems catch up.

- [Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928)](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)
- [Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263)](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)
